questions.question: What is the definitive host in the life cycle of Echinococcus granulosus?
questions.explanation: The dog is the definitive host, harboring the adult worm in its small intestine and eliminating eggs with feces.

questions.question: Which organ of the sheep primarily houses the larval form of Echinococcus granulosus?
questions.explanation: The liver and lungs are the most common sites of hydatid cyst formation in sheep, the intermediate host.

questions.question: How does the dog become infected in the biological cycle of hydatidosis?
questions.explanation: The dog becomes infected by feeding on sheep viscera that contain fertile hydatid cysts with protoscolices.

questions.question: What structure of the parasite is eliminated by the dog and contaminates the environment?
questions.explanation: The dog eliminates embryonated eggs of Echinococcus granulosus in its feces, which contaminate the grass and the environment.

questions.question: What larval stage develops in sheep after ingesting Echinococcus granulosus eggs?
questions.explanation: The hydatid or hydatid cyst is the larval phase that forms in the tissues of the sheep, containing protoscolices.

questions.question: What is the main measure to interrupt the dog-sheep cycle of hydatid disease?
options: Do not feed dogs raw organ meats
questions.explanation: Preventing dogs from consuming raw sheep viscera breaks the cycle by preventing the parasite from completing its development in the definitive host.

questions.question: What is the most common location of hydatid cyst in adults?
questions.explanation: The right liver is the most common location of hydatid cysts in adults, representing approximately 60-70% of cases, due to portal leakage of Echinococcus granulosus embryos.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for an uncomplicated hepatic hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: The treatment of choice for uncomplicated hepatic hydatid cysts is the PAIR technique (puncture, aspiration, injection and reaspiration) combined with albendazole, since it offers efficacy with less morbidity than surgery.

questions.question: Which ultrasound finding is characteristic of a type CE1 hepatic hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: The double-walled ultrasound image (double line sign) is characteristic of the CE1 type hydatid cyst, corresponding to a unilocular cyst with visible wall of the pericystium and endocyst.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of hepatic hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: The main pathophysiological mechanism is chronic granulomatous inflammation triggered by the hydatid cyst, which leads to fibrosis and eventual calcification, although it can also cause mechanical and allergic complications.

questions.question: What is the first-line serological test to confirm hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: The Western blot IgG is the first-line confirmatory serological test, with high specificity (>95%) for hydatidosis, allowing the differentiation of active past infection and avoiding false positive ELISA results.

questions.question: What acute complication can a right hepatic hydatid cyst present?
questions.explanation: Bile duct rupture is the most common acute complication of right hepatic hydatid cyst, manifesting as obstructive cholangitis or jaundice, and may require cholangiography and endoscopic drainage.

questions.question: Why is the Casoni test currently out of use?
options: Risk of anaphylaxis and low accuracy
questions.explanation: The Casoni test, an intradermorreaction with hydatid antigen, was abandoned due to the risk of severe anaphylactic reactions and its low sensitivity and specificity, and was replaced by serological and imaging methods.

questions.question: What type of immediate reaction does the Casoni test evaluate?
questions.explanation: The Casoni test is based on an immediate hypersensitivity reaction (type I), mediated by IgE, which produces a papule and erythema 15-20 minutes after the intradermal injection of hydatid antigen.

questions.question: Which of the following methods replaced Casoni's test?
questions.explanation: The Casoni test was replaced by serological techniques such as ELISA and Western blot, which detect specific antibodies against Echinococcus granulosus, offering greater safety and diagnostic precision without the risk of anaphylaxis.

questions.question: What antigen was used in the Casoni test?
questions.explanation: The Casoni test used crude somatic antigen obtained from hydatid cysts of Echinococcus granulosus, injected intradermally to provoke an immediate hypersensitivity reaction.

questions.question: What is an absolute contraindication of the Casoni test?
questions.explanation: The Casoni test is contraindicated in patients with a history of anaphylaxis, due to the risk of triggering a new severe allergic reaction due to the injection of hydatid antigen, which led to its abandonment.

questions.question: What diagnostic limitation did the Casoni test present?
questions.explanation: The Casoni test presented high cross-reactivity with other parasitic infections (such as taeniasis or schistosomiasis), generating false positives, which, added to its low sensitivity and specificity, justified its disuse in clinical practice.

questions.question: What is the characteristic radiological finding of chronic hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Ring calcification is a classic radiological sign of chronic hepatic hydatid disease, indicating calcification of the hydatid cyst wall.

questions.question: What anatomical structure calcifies in chronic hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: In chronic hepatic hydatid disease, ring calcification corresponds to calcification of the hydatid cyst wall, not the parenchyma or bile ducts.

questions.question: What is another name for ring calcification in hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Ring calcification is also known as the eggshell sign, due to its characteristic radiological appearance in chronic hepatic hydatid disease.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of ring calcification on an abdominal x-ray?
questions.explanation: The most common cause of ring calcification on abdominal radiography is chronic hepatic hydatid cyst, due to calcification of its cystic wall.

questions.question: What type of calcification does a chronic hepatic hydatid cyst present?
questions.explanation: Ring calcification is the typical form of calcification of chronic hepatic hydatid cyst, surrounding the lesion as a radiopaque ring.

questions.question: Which radiological finding suggests inactivity of a hepatic hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: Complete ring calcification of the cyst wall indicates biological inactivity of the hydatid cyst, being a sign of chronic evolution and a dead cyst.

questions.question: What is the main indication for surgery plus albendazole in hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Combined surgery with albendazole is indicated in large cysts (greater than 5 cm) or complicated (ruptured, infected, biliary), where the risk of complications is greater and cyst removal is required.

questions.question: What albendazole regimen is used preoperatively in programmed hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: The recommended regimen is albendazole 10-15 mg/kg/day for 28 days before surgery, to reduce the viability of the parasite and the risk of intraoperative dissemination.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of albendazole in hydatidosis?
questions.explanation: Albendazole acts by inhibiting tubulin polymerization, which interferes with the formation of microtubules, affecting glucose uptake and parasite viability.

questions.question: What postsurgical complication of hepatic hydatid disease is prevented with albendazole?
questions.explanation: Pre- and postoperative albendazole reduces the viability of protoscolices, reducing the risk of secondary peritoneal seeding during surgical manipulation of the cyst.

questions.question: What is the total duration of treatment with albendazole in uncomplicated hydatid disease after surgery?
questions.explanation: After surgery, it is recommended to continue albendazole for at least 3 months to eliminate possible residual cysts or microscopic dissemination, with monitoring of liver function.

questions.question: What finding contraindicates the use of albendazole as a surgical adjuvant in hydatidosis?
questions.explanation: Albendazole is contraindicated in pregnancy due to its teratogenic and embryotoxic potential, so in these cases surgical treatment without albendazole or alternative management is evaluated.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of a hydatid cyst rupture?
questions.explanation: Rupture of a hydatid cyst releases parasite antigens into the bloodstream, triggering a systemic anaphylactic reaction that can be rapidly fatal if untreated.

questions.question: What primary mediator causes anaphylaxis in hydatid cyst rupture?
questions.explanation: Hydatid cyst antigens activate IgE-sensitized mast cells, releasing histamine and other mediators that produce vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and anaphylactic shock.

questions.question: Which of the following findings is characteristic of anaphylaxis due to hydatid cyst rupture?
questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis presents with urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm and rapid hypotension; The combination of urticaria and bronchospasm is a clinical hallmark of acute allergic reaction.

questions.question: What first-line pharmacological treatment should be administered when suspected anaphylaxis due to ruptured hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: Intramuscular adrenaline is the drug of choice in anaphylaxis due to its rapid vasoconstrictor and bronchodilator effect, reversing shock and bronchospasm.

questions.question: What is the immediate pathophysiological mechanism of shock in hydatid cyst rupture?
options: Intra-abdominal hemorrhage due to vascular erosion
Direct myocardial depression due to parasitic toxins
Massive peripheral vasodilation and increased capillary permeability
Obstruction of the inferior vena cava by the cyst
questions.explanation: The release of histamine and other mediators produces systemic vasodilation and increased capillary permeability, causing relative hypovolemia and circulatory collapse.

questions.question: What factor increases the risk of rupture of a hepatic hydatid cyst?
options: Complete calcification of the cyst wall
questions.explanation: Blunt abdominal trauma can mechanically rupture the wall of the hydatid cyst, releasing its antigenic content and triggering anaphylaxis.

questions.question: What is the pathognomonic finding of a unilocular hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: The hydatid grit, composed of protoscólices and hooks, is the characteristic and pathognomonic microscopic finding of the unilocular hydatid cyst, indicating viability of the parasite.

questions.question: What structure of the hydatid cyst gives rise to hydatid grit?
questions.explanation: The germinative or proliferative membrane is the inner layer of the cyst that produces the protoscolices and hooks, which when detached form the hydatid grit.

questions.question: What is the most common location of unilocular hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: The liver, especially the right lobe, is the most common location of hydatid cysts (approximately 60-70% of cases), followed by the lung.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for an uncomplicated unilocular hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: Surgical resection (cystipericystectomy) remains the treatment of choice for uncomplicated unilocular hydatid cysts, as it allows complete removal of the parasite and reduces the risk of recurrence.

questions.question: What parasite causes unilocular hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: Echinococcus granulosus is the causative agent of the unilocular hydatid cyst (cystic hydatid disease), while Echinococcus multilocularis produces the multilocular alveolar form.

questions.question: What imaging finding is characteristic of unilocular hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: Eggshell calcification or peripheral calcification is a typical imaging finding of chronic or dead hydatid cysts, visible on plain radiography and computed tomography.

questions.question: What is the most sensitive serological test for the initial diagnosis of hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: ELISA IgG is the most sensitive serological test for the initial diagnosis of hydatid disease, detecting specific antibodies against Echinococcus granulosus.

questions.question: On liver ultrasound, what classification is used for hydatid cysts?
questions.explanation: The Gharbi classification is the most used in ultrasound to categorize hepatic hydatid cysts according to their morphology and content.

questions.question: What ultrasound sign is characteristic of a complicated hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: The double layer sign (detachment of the germinative membrane) is characteristic of a complicated or degenerating hydatid cyst.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of ELISA in the diagnosis of hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: ELISA exhibits cross-reactivity with other cestode infections (such as taeniasis), which reduces its specificity and requires confirmatory testing.

questions.question: On ultrasound, which finding suggests an inactive hydatid cyst?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound mural calcification indicates an inactive or dead hydatid cyst, with a low probability of complications and without the need for surgical treatment.

questions.question: What antibody does the ELISA for hydatidosis mainly detect?
questions.explanation: ELISA for hydatidosis primarily detects specific IgG against Echinococcus granulosus antigens, indicating exposure or active infection.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of secondary peritoneal hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Secondary peritoneal hydatidosis occurs mainly due to the rupture of a hepatic hydatid cyst, releasing protoscolices that seed the peritoneum.

questions.question: What imaging finding is characteristic of secondary peritoneal hydatid disease?
options: Multiple cystic lesions in the peritoneum
questions.explanation: The presence of multiple cysts in the peritoneum, often in different evolutionary stages, is the typical finding after rupture of a liver cyst.

questions.question: What surgical complication is most associated with secondary peritoneal hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Intraoperative or spontaneous rupture of the cyst can release hydatid antigens into the bloodstream, triggering a severe anaphylactic reaction.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for secondary peritoneal hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Complete surgical resection of all peritoneal cysts is the treatment of choice, combined with pre- and postoperative albendazole to reduce recurrences.

questions.question: What parasite causes secondary peritoneal hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Echinococcus granulosus is the causative agent of cystic hydatid disease; Its rupture can spread protoscolices to the peritoneum, generating secondary seeding.

questions.question: What is the most common clinical manifestation of secondary peritoneal hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: Diffuse abdominal pain is the most common symptom, due to peritoneal irritation caused by multiple cysts disseminated in the cavity.

questions.question: What does the acronym PAIR mean in the treatment of hydatid cysts?
questions.explanation: PAIR corresponds to Puncture, Aspiration, Injection of a scolicidal agent and Reaspiration of the contents, being a minimally invasive technique for inaccessible cysts.

questions.question: What is the main indication of the PAIR technique in hepatic hydatid disease?
questions.explanation: The PAIR technique is indicated mainly in hydatid cysts inaccessible for surgery, especially those type CE1 and CE3a, reducing surgical risks.

questions.question: What scolicidal agent is injected during the PAIR technique?
questions.explanation: 20-30% hypertonic saline solution is used as a scolicidal agent, injected after aspiration of the cystic contents to destroy protoscolices.

questions.question: What is an absolute contraindication to performing PAIR?
questions.explanation: Biliary communication is an absolute contraindication due to the risk of sclerosing cholangitis when injecting scolicide, which can pass into the biliary tree.

questions.question: What complication is most frequently associated with the PAIR technique?
questions.explanation: The most common complication is fever and mild abdominal pain, generally due to an inflammatory reaction or secondary infection, manageable with analgesia and antibiotics.

questions.question: What type of hydatid cyst is ideal for applying PAIR?
questions.explanation: Type CE1 (unilocular) and CE3a (with membrane detachment) cysts are ideal due to their liquid content and absence of thick septa.
